sentences.text: Yes.
It's-- I think there were more people than they could cope with.
It just happened, it happened occasionally, which I know now.
Because I stayed there.
That too many transports arrived at one go.
And when I arrived that was the case.
It was too many people.
So I spoke to my father, and he kind of looked at me.
And he said, you are going to survive.
And he said it with such-- like a command.
And then he disappeared.
And I went my own way.
And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we went to-- we went to the shower.
And the SS said we had to have head shaved, and-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and that's when you first time feel like you become an animal, you become dehumanized.
And from then on, you just follow instructions and orders.
First, you don't quite realize.
Because you don't believe it.
And you think in 10 minutes it will turn.
It will pass.
It will change.
And you know, you're going to this shower, hundreds of girls in the same room having a shower.
And you don't recognize people.
Because they've all been shaved, and-- and you are so numb and so frightened that you don't think.
You-- you just follow whatever is, you know, you don't even hear the instructions.
You don't hear the command.
You just follow the crowd.
Anyhow, we got to the uniform, and we are located.
We had to walk to-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] where we slept.
What was it called?
The bunker?
Pardon?
The bunker.
But it was not where we arrived.
It was another place.
Yeah.
Birkenau, sorry.
Thank you.
And that's where we got our bunkers allocated.
It was gray and navy stripes, just an ordinary-- a straight dress, which we didn't wear after a time.
But I'll tell you about that.
Yes, on the pocket, I think.
And we tried to kind of everybody finding somebody they knew or recognized and keep together.
And it was very bad because I had nobody.
Where sisters kept together and some odd mother here and there with a child, you know.
So anyhow, I found some friend.
And we bunked into the same thing.
In the morning, we were woken up by the kapos.
It was-- I don't know, 3 o'clock.
We all had to line up, which was called the Zahlappell, you know, for counting, in rows of four and lines of four, standing there for hours still, till somebody came to count us.
It was very, very, very, very cold.
And then we were allocated a job, which I stayed till I was in Auschwitz.
It was a place called Kanada.
Now Kanada, it was nicknamed Kanada, because it was a richer type of place.
Because it was near the crematorium.
And we took the belongings of the people who went into the crematorium to sort.
I was on the sorting commando, which meant any clothes or anything which came into the camp went to this particular place where I worked.
And we were sorting, the dishes, some shoes, you know, sorting out all the-- but it was near the crematorium.
And sometimes we had to go to the crematorium to pick up the belongings.
And after a few months, we stayed there.
We even slept there.
We didn't have to walk to the other camp.
And that was our job.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And it was hard work, because it was long hours.
And it was night shifts, three weeks night and three weeks day.
And when you're hungry, and you're under-nourished, and you're young, to be up at night is torture, absolute torture.
Because you fall asleep.
Whatever you do for sleep.
And sorting clothes which have just come out of these heated drums, you know, the disinfectant.
So the smell and the heat, you see, that was good.
Because we weren't cold.
But it made you go to sleep.
And if you're found sleeping, we were beaten to-- to death, not to death, because we aren't allowed to kill us at that point.
So you keep each other going, you know, wake up, wake up, wake up, and we always had somebody standing on the side, seeing if somebody's coming, and then we started [NON-ENGLISH].
It means rain.
And if a girls are screaming [NON-ENGLISH], I mean we knew that.
But if you were asleep, you didn't hear it.
And many times I got very, very, very beaten, badly beaten.
And that was very, very hard too.
Were the girls that you were working with, your age?
No.
I was the youngest.
In this particular camp, I was the youngest.
Which in a way it was good.
Because I had certain little privileges.
If I was cold, I wasn't so badly beaten.
I have been beaten a lot, but not-- because I had to get on myself, there were a few elderly people which now probably they were 30, elderly.
And I found I wanted to look after them, and-- because I had the guts.
If you are 15, you gave guts.
At 17, 18, I don't think you have as much guts, because you think logically.
I didn't.
So I always managed to pinch bread, or a bit of water, or whatever.
So I was caught more times than I should have been caught.
And one particular time, I was taken to the marketplace, and there was a pile of sticks, you know, with the sorting, there was a pile of sticks.
And he broke three walking sticks on me.
I was so ill.
I had to lie on my stomach in hospital for three weeks, which sounds very dramatic.
You know, hospital.
It wasn't a hospital.
It was just a bunker which was kept for people that had-- see certain, this time in the beginning, the people were numbered, they kind of sort.
They might have to account for.
So in the beginning they didn't select our transport.
They just let us be.
Because they, for instance, kept books of people who arrived.
And we had to sign, died of typhoid, and died of diphtheria, whatever.
Because they covered themself in case there will be a time when they have to account.
So in the beginning, we weren't selected like later on.
So they had to account for us.
They couldn't-- if you died naturally, fine.
But he couldn't quite beat me long enough to-- he managed to beat me till-- till nearly.
And I-- we worked-- I worked there till January, watching the transport coming and listening to the screaming.
These were certain transports which came from Poland.
They all came through us.
They had to come through our gate.
And certain transport, like transport from the ghettos in Poland, were the most horrific transport.
Because they knew what the chimneys were for.
And if you put them into the shower or into the crematorium, it made no difference.
They just screamed.
They wouldn't go in.
So they were beaten in.
And to listen to that, you know, and in the meantime outside the gate, there was these musicians playing the music.
And the screams were-- when the screams stopped, it was a relief.
Even so we knew this 1,000, 2,000 people at that moment were gassed.
But it was a relief for us and for them.
But sometimes at night just during the day wasn't so bad.
But at night to listen to the scream and [NON-ENGLISH], it was really.
[CRYING]
I was there till January.
And then they collected us, and they started marching us.
Yeah?
Yeah, not inside, not right inside.
It's just where they got undressed.
I had to collect the clothes, what they took off.
What did what--
Just people going through.
At one point, I met my grandmother.
And she says to me, you know, I haven't heard from Mommy in a long time.
Where is Mommy, you know, her daughter, my mother.
And I don't know how I managed, but I said, don't worry.
You just go in here, and she's over there.
Which-- [CRYING]
Well, they all perished, them too, in the crematorium, it seems.
I mean I never had proof.
And I never saw them.
But that was the natural-- that what happened.
And my grandmother, I saw her going in.
But not my father.
My father went to work, it seems.
And he worked for about six months in a different camp.
I don't know which camp.
And it seems he came back to Auschwitz too, to go to the crematorium.
He had an accident in a factory.
He was working with wagons in a station, in a railway station.
And he trapped his arm between two wagons.
And it seems his arm was taken off.
And he came to Auschwitz, I think roundabout August.
And I think I saw him.
But it would have been my imagination.
I can't prove that I saw him there.
Because somebody send me a message.
Come.
You're father is here, you know near in the barrack, where they collected to go to the crematorium.
And I looked in.
And I thought I saw him.
But either I didn't want to see him, because I knew where he was going, or maybe it wasn't him.
I don't know.
But I was told it was him.
And I stayed there till January.
That's what I was doing all the time.
We had our ration, a bowl of food-- a bowl of-- call it muck, whatever, soup, and a cup of coffee, black coffee, and a piece of bread.
But I must say that sometimes we were lucky.
Because when we were sorting the clothes, in some pockets we sometimes found a little bit of bread or something.
So that's why it's Kanada, because Kanada was described as the rich land.
So our company was called Kanada.
Because there was rich picking.
I mean if you found peace of bread, it was rich picking.
And also the diamonds and the jewelry, what went through our hands was tremendous.
And our aim was never to give it to the Germans.
So whenever if we found them, either we flushed them down the toilet or dug them somewhere.
And the men used to come from another barrack, another camp to collect the stuff.
And we managed to kind of give it to them that they should either dispose of it, we couldn't do anything with it.
Diamonds, I can't tell you the amount of diamonds and gold and jewelry which was hidden.
And that was our job, to sort it and find whatever was sewn into these.
I once found a dressing gown, buttoned through from top to the bottom.
Every button was a diamond, it was covered with a material, pretending it's buttons.
I don't know how-- one of the girls said, you know, they're not even, these buttons.
And they were all diamonds.
And at one point, one of the men send us a message that we should try to keep the diamonds, whichever way we should keep it, not that we thought we will survive, but maybe not to give it to them.
We did our utmost, it was our aim just to get rid of it, not to give it to the Germans.
They want your old clothes and the dirty pots and pans, fine.
But just not to give them the valuables.
Sometimes we had to.
Because it was suspicious.
They knew.
So occasionally, we made a little pile of gold or something and gave it to them.
But if it was really valuable, then we tried to get rid of it.
So one day this-- one of the men came to put the-- we used to put them on sheets, put the clothes in, and tie them.
And these bandages-- bundles went of top of the [INAUDIBLE], which was filled with-- by demand.
Well, we weren't allowed to talk to each other.
We weren't allowed to go near.
Everything was in distance.
No people were allowed to kind of meet.
Anyway, one of these men told us in Yiddish that we should.
So he said that every day when he comes, we should give him a pair of our shoes.
And we did.
And he put in each of the heals, a diamond.
He build it in.
He a shoemaker.
It sounds like-- I mean, we had a wonderful time, and the shoemaker came.
It's not that type of thing.
But we managed somehow to do that.
So a lot of us had a bit of diamond on us, which didn't mean anything at the time, and didn't mean for a long term anything.
And it was-- compared to some of the girls, it wasn't such a bad time.
Because we had that little bit of food.
But emotionally, it was much worse.
But-- emotion didn't come into it at the time.
You know, when you try to survive, and try to protect your physical health, and like if you fight for your own stomach and you're very hungry, you don't get involved with emotions.
I used to say God must be wonderful, because he killed my parents, and killed my brother.
And they don't have to go through this.
I was grateful at the time.
It-- it was wonderful.
It was-- if you ever believed in God that was where you believed in God that he's good.
Because-- [CRYING] you see watching people go in, who were mainly great people, old people, children.
So we considered ourselves as the sinners.
Because who were we?
Why us?
So we thought we were here for-- because we don't deserve to die.
See, if you were great, you died.
Because if you didn't die, you suffered.
And-- on the other hand, you didn't commit suicide.
Because you can't commit suicide.
It's not something-- lots of people did.
But I mean, we just wished to die.
On the other hand, you've got that seventh sense, where you protect yourself from death.
You protect your life.
It's automatically, however much you hope you'll go to sleep and never wake up.
But you wake up, and then you struggle along again for that piece of bread.
So when I was working in Auschwitz, it was fantastic that people you loved weren't suffering.
That was a bonus to me, to all of us.
I mean I wasn't unique.
Because I was watching sometimes mothers and daughters.
Their love killed each other, you know, pushing the bread.
No, you have it.
I have it.
You have it.
I have it.
And by the time they finished, the bread was either dropped or it was too late, or the SS came in.
You know, this-- and I was saying, thank God my mother is not here.
Thank God my mother-- and that's all I was thinking of, thanking God they are not suffering.
And-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and then there was a the couple of uprisings, where there was once a crematorium uprising.
Now for only one night, there was very big-- the sirens blowing, and everybody, and there were a lot of noises.
And all of a sudden, men were running into our barracks, saying, hide us, hide us.
There is an uprising.
So we hid them like in these big piles of the sorted things.
We used to bury them in there.
But it didn't work.
It's just-- it didn't.
They managed to get them and kill them, and make another commandant, and the crematorium went on again.
And-- bug for a few days, all of us were in terrible danger.
Because we tried to hide them.
It didn't matter.
It didn't-- you see we were going to die.
This was a question of when and how.
Because those people who knew too much were the first to be killed, like the commandos in the-- they were called commandos in the crematorium, they're changed every three months.
So they killed them, and got another one.
They didn't want them to know too much, in case they escape, or in case.
So we were categorized in that thing.
We won't survive.
Because we know too much.
So uprising, [INAUDIBLE] uprising.
We're going to die anyhow.
And, you know, it's very hard to explain how logic or emotion doesn't come into it.
You're fighting for your life.
You're fighting for a piece of bread.
You don't think, now what do I think is happening?
It's happening.
You're glad today you weren't beaten.
You're glad yesterday you had a mouthful more bread.
You-- you don't speculate.
So this one was run onto the wire and got killed.
Fine, she's [INAUDIBLE].
She's lucky.
I'm not lucky.
Because I haven't got the guts to do it.
So that is the mentality.
You know, that's how you live.
You're not a human.
You're dehumanized.
You don't think.
You follow instructions like an animal, not even-- an animal wouldn't listen to it.
So you become a piece of robot really, which if you don't where emotions and hunger meets or takeover.
So we fought for our lives and-- and then in January, we started-- they said the Russians are coming, and we have to-- they didn't say Russians are coming.
They said, everybody line up and we'll walk.
But lots of people were lucky.
Because they-- it wasn't time to collect them and take them.
So a lot of people stayed in Auschwitz.
And they evacuated already in January.
That's right.
Nine months.
Yeah.
With me?
Yeah, not many, very, very few, very, very few.
Some of them stayed and survived then straightaway.
Which the percentage of our lot, the survival rate, went down from then on.
Most of my friends and my relations, whoever was on that walk, died on that walk, or died after the evacuation.
That was the most horrific time.
For me, it wasn't the Auschwitz time.
It was this time, which become much, much worse.
And from there, not many of us came back.
But maybe four or five.
I remember we got back to Prague, 13 of us out of a thousand from my group.
And I think a lot even died in Prague.
Because they [INAUDIBLE], and that was wrong.
No, no.
Because we had no dates, we wouldn't have had dates.
And maybe in the very beginning we used to say, now, I think it's Friday night.
And then we used to sit down and discuss what we used to eat, and what we are going to eat.
And everybody gave their menu, and talked a lot about food.
I think it's one of the things you talk about when you're hungry.
And you know, we used to have [INAUDIBLE].
No, we used to have something else.
You know, it was-- and that was a time passing, you know, who had what at this time of the day.
And then we would stand up.
I make a promise, when I get out of here, I will only eat this and this.
I don't want the chicken soup.
I don't really like chicken soup.
I don't really like the fish.
We just have bread, you know like trying to come and compromise when we get back, knowing that we won't get back.
And then we started walking.
Conditions were very bad.
Anybody who tumbled or slowed down, got shot.
Because realizing later, they didn't want to be caught.
The SS didn't want to be caught.
So they had to drive us faster and faster.
So we walked through the snow, and the rain, and the cold.
January cold in Poland is not quite pleasant.
And we went to one lager.
There was no room.
We stayed two or three days sleeping outside.
Then we went to another one, on and on and on, till we hit Berlin.
And those lagers were overcrowded, no food.
Conditions were outrageous.
And they kept on driving us further and further away from the-- I think we were running away from the Russians.
And then we-- then we-- they got us into trains.
We were sleeping on the streets, in the snow, not be able to get up anymore.
Just leaving people, half dead on the street.
Once or twice, we managed to get into some hay barns.
And we slept there, managed to find little pea potatoes.
We had nothing to eat, nothing.
And we ate snow.
If-- if a tree, had a green bit of thing, we picked it and ate it.
And how one survived that, I don't know, if it was a miracle or if it was my father's words, the will to go on, I can't tell you.
And then we came to a place called [INAUDIBLE], which is in Germany.
And they needed people to dig air raid shelters.
How they expected us to dig air raid shelter, when we couldn't even lift up a knife or fork, or a piece of wood.
But that was-- they had nowhere to take us anymore.
There was just no time, and nowhere to take us.
April, or March, March, either March or April.
Anyway they got us there.
And they gave us two rooms for that whole lot of us, soup once a day, nowhere to lie down.
There wasn't enough.
Infested with lice, I mean in the morning, we were black covered with lice.
We had dysentery, typhoid.
And we were just dying, one by one-- not one by one.
I mean in the morning if you-- when the light came up, half the room was dead.
And that's how it went.
It was-- it was horrendous.
And one day I was out digging this-- supposed to be digging this air raid shelter.
And I couldn't lift-- lift up the shovel.
It was ridiculous.
I think they just found this job to keep us going.
And a fellow said to me, what is your biggest wish in life?
I said, salt.
And he said, OK.
I'll bring you salt.
And the next day, he brought me a little packet of salt.
I didn't ask for bread.
[CRYING] Why did you want salt?
I think the craving for something, it was salt.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I think if you run out of salt in your body, the craving is so-- anyhow, he gave me salt.
And then I asked him, why was he good to me?
He was an SS.
He said because I've been away in the army-- I don't know-- seven or eight years.
And I left a daughter your age.
So, I think that he had some kind of-- [NON-ENGLISH], [? as they call it. ?] And he gave me this salt.
And then I started to talk to him.
I said, how could I get some soup?
I've got a diamond.
And he said, OK.
I'll see that you get soup tomorrow.
And I managed to scrape out this diamond from my shoe.
And he gave me-- a can of soup for this huge, big diamond.
And then I think a week later, he brought me something.
I think, a piece of bread.
And I gave him another piece of diamond.
Because to me, it meant nothing.
And after that, we just stayed.
Nobody could move.
Nobody could get up.
Nobody could dig these air raid shelters.
So we just stayed in the room dying.
And whoever was alive, pulled the dead ones up, and put them on a heap.
It was like a mountain of bodies.
OK.
